ceu53.title: Psychosocial Wellbeing Assessment Tools for Children with Cerebral Palsy and Their Families:  A Rapid Review
ceu53.description: Cerebral palsy is a lifelong motor diagnosis which is primarily associated with impaired movement, balance, and posture. Early intervention programs are widely encouraged to alleviate the physical, psychosocial, social and economic burden of these functional challenges. Despite the benefit of these early interventions, there are no standardised quantitative assessment measures or guidelines as to how to measure the benefits. We conducted and now report a rapid review of the applications of quantitative psychosocial wellbeing assessment tools for children with cerebral palsy under six and their families between 2017 and 2025. This rapid review was done as part of a charity, funded project to measure and assess their early interventions. After searching PsycINFO, Medline, CINAHL databases we identified and extracted data from 113 articles and assessed the utility of 34 free to access psychometric tests identified within them. While this review helped us to identify measures for our charity project, we also identify inconsistencies with reporting theoretical models and the factor validity of current measures, critique the lack of transparency in the data collection procedures reported and commiserate the number of inaccessible, paywalled measures that impair the measurement and assessment of wellbeing of children with cerebral palsy and their families.

3ed7s.title: Resilience across pandemic phases: distinguishing psychosocial correlates from stress-buffering effects in youth
3ed7s.description:
Adolescence and young adulthood are sensitive developmental periods in which stress exposure increases. Youth differ in their capacity to maintain or regain mental health after stressful experiences, reflecting individual differences in resilience. Resilience resources can support mental health by promoting resilient functioning and/or by buffering the association between stress exposure and mental health. The COVID-19 pandemic provides a unique context to investigate these processes across changing stress exposures.
	We examined youth resilience before, during, and after the COVID-19 pandemic in large Dutch population-based samples, including youth aged 12-25 years before (N = 12,937, 58% female), during (N = 8,934, 58% female), and after the pandemic (N = 37,797, 62% female). We investigated whether loneliness, optimism, and self-rated health were associated with resilient functioning and, to test stress-buffering effects, whether they moderated associations between stress exposure and mental health. Resilient functioning was operationalised as better-than-expected mental health given stress exposure, based on wellbeing, depressive symptoms, and a combination. 
	Higher optimism, lower loneliness, and better self-rated health were consistently associated with higher resilient functioning across pandemic phases and operationalisations (p<.001). Moderation analyses showed inconsistent evidence that these characteristics buffered associations between stress exposure and mental health. 
	Psychosocial characteristics may help explain which youth function better in the context of stress, but do not consistently indicate who is less affected by stress. The results support a view of resilience as a complex and context-dependent process, rather than the result of single protective factors with similar effects across time and contexts.

6ypwr.title: Depressive Symptom Trajectories Around the Retirement Transition Across Countries and Historical Periods
6ypwr.description: As global aging accelerates, retirement has become a critical transition in older adults’ lives, with profound implications for mental health. Depression trajectories around retirement are inconsistent, with some studies suggesting worsening symptoms and others indicating improvement. Using HRS (United States, 1994–2020), SHARE (Europe and Israel, 2004–2022), CHARLS (China, 2011–2020), and KLoSA (South Korea, 2006–2020), this study examined depressive symptom trajectories around retirement. We emphasized three key components of the retirement transition: retirement age, pre-retirement phase, and post-retirement phase. Using multilevel models, we estimated changes in depressive symptoms around retirement transition, accounting for cross-national and historical differences. Results showed that depressive symptom trajectories varied across datasets and historical periods, but average changes were generally modest. HRS showed pre-retirement increases, an immediate rise at retirement, and post-retirement decline. SHARE showed mainly a small immediate decrease, CHARLS showed a steady increase across the retirement transition, and KLoSA showed an immediate increase followed by post-retirement decline. Period-specific analyses showed further variation, although many trajectories remained close to stable; in CHARLS, later retirement periods showed steeper post-retirement increases. The findings show that retirement-related changes in depressive symptoms are often modest at the group level, while small departures from stability may vary by temporal phase and historical context.

pkwxy.title: Assessing chronic pain processes longitudinally: Psychometric evaluation of the German “Assessing Chronic Pain Processes” (ProCEss) item set
pkwxy.description:
Background. Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) is gaining relevance in contemporaneous pain research. However, EMA questionnaires are rarely psychometrically evaluated, potentially compromising data quality. This study aimed to develop and evaluate a German item set assessing psychological chronic pain processes for use in EMA, focusing on variability and reliability. 
Methods. First, the item set was piloted with three people suffering from chronic pain to gather feedback on participant burden and the usability of the data. Second, an evaluation was conducted with a sample of n = 68 individuals with lived experience of chronic pain who completed an EMA protocol with six assessments per day over twelve days (4,236 datapoints). Third, revised parts of the item set were preliminarily evaluated in n = 10 people suffering from chronic pain over a 21-day EMA period with six assessments per day (1,253 datapoints). Variability was examined primarily using within-person standard deviations and response distributions. Reliability was assessed according to current recommendations for single-item measures (test-retest reliability), two-item scales (Spearman-Brown coefficient), and multi-item scales (McDonald’s Omega).
Results. Most items and process scales met the predefined criteria for variability and reliability. However, variance was largely attributable to between-person differences. Four out of thirteen scales demonstrated insufficient reliability.
Conclusions. This study presents the first psychometrically evaluated German EMA item set capturing a broad range of clinically relevant pain processes. While the findings are promising, further refinement of several scales and additional psychometric evaluation are warranted.

mgbq2.title: Sensitive periods for childhood adversity and their impact on later life outcomes: A narrative review
mgbq2.description:
Rationale. Childhood adversity can impair mental and physical health later in life, and these deleterious effects may depend on sensitive periods for child abuse during which children are particularly vulnerable. The purpose of this narrative review is to scope previous research on sensitive periods of child adversity that are linked with negative later life outcomes.
Methods. Only peer-reviewed original research articles were included in the review. Relevant key information was extracted from the articles and themes were identified as repeated patterns of meaning-making that evolve around a similar interpretation of the research findings.
Findings. From the scoped literature on sensitive periods for childhood adversity, three themes were identified: (1) The earlier the childhood adversity in life the worse, (2) There are two sensitive periods that correspond to periods of change in development: early childhood and early adolescence. (3) Sensitive periods for child adversity are domain-specific and depend on the aspect of impaired psychosocial functioning.
Conclusions. Despite evidence for sensitive periods for child adversity, it is too early to describe normative, sensitive periods because previous findings were largely study-specific. More research is needed to investigate the impact of child abuse on various domains of psychosocial functioning later in life. More prospective, and longitudinal research is also needed because current findings are subject to retrospective memory bias. Overall, when child abuse occurred during the lifespan may be important information in optimising and personalising treatment options.

4nh62.title: Lost in aggregation: from score-based to representational mentalization
4nh62.description:
Psychological measures are commonly reduced to aggregate scores, such as questionnaire totals, accuracy scores, or human-annotated measures. Although useful, these scores may hide information about the configuration of participants’ responses. The present study took mentalization (i.e., the ability to infer others’ mental states) as a working example to investigate how this domain can be characterized through vector-based response representations in addition to classical scoring. One hundred and sixty-four university participants completed the Interpersonal Reactivity Index (IRI), the Reading the Mind in the Eyes Test (RMET), and the Triangles task. For each task, we constructed participant-by-participant distance matrices based on classical scores and on vector representations: IRI item-response profiles, RMET response-choice patterns, and sentence embeddings of open-ended Triangles ToM descriptions. Classical score correlations showed limited convergence across tasks, with only RMET accuracy and Triangles score showing a small positive association. Representational similarity analyses showed that vector-based representations reliably converged with classical score distances within each task, although the associations were modest. Cross-task analyses further indicated that score-based and vector-based representations produced partly different prediction patterns. Overall, these findings suggest that classical mentalization measures organize individual differences differently depending on whether responses are represented as aggregate scores or full response configurations, with implications for how psychologists conceptualize and validate social-cognitive assessment.
Keywords: mentalization; representational similarity analysis; individual differences.

jxg3n.title: Intervention-Induced Neuroticism Change Across Multiple Timescales
jxg3n.description: Neuroticism is one of the most consequential personality traits for individuals and society. Interventions that facilitate changes in this trait may thus have far-reaching benefits, with initial evidence supporting that interventions can foster lasting neuroticism change. Most personality theories posit that changes in broad, enduring traits are mediated via lower-level changes in momentary states and intermediary habits. However, there are few direct tests of this theorized bottom-up pathway, leaving the mechanisms, direction, and timing of effects unclear. Using data from the Changing How I Live Life Study (N = 444), which evaluated a digital intervention to reduce neuroticism, we examined 1) the shape and magnitude of intervention-induced neuroticism change across three timescales, 2) concurrent and lagged associations among these changes, and 3) whether these associations varied across treatment groups receiving strategies targeting different neuroticism levels. We found that neuroticism changed nonlinearly across the intervention; changes in momentary, daily, and weekly neuroticism measures reliably predicted one another, with bottom-up effects often being larger in magnitude; and mean-level changes and coupled changes in momentary, daily, and weekly neuroticism measures were larger in the intervention groups than in the active control group. These results support the conceptual and causal importance of both bottom-up and top-down (momentary, specific ⟷ long-term, broad) change processes. Together, this study tested key theoretical predictions about the process and directionality of intervention-induced personality change to help elucidate mechanisms underlying intentional personality changes and generate insights about how to enhance intervention efficacy.

7ehdm.title: Impact of a Pre-Test Measurement of Alcohol Craving: Lessons From a Self-Replication Study
7ehdm.description:
A prior study during COVID-19 lockdown (Heck et al., 2024) examined whether a pre-test alcohol craving measurement influenced posttest assessment and tested its relationship with social desirability (SD) and demand effects (the consequences of participants trying to uncover the study’s aim). Results suggested that the first craving measurement increased its post-experimental levels in heavy drinkers, with no associations found for SD or demand effects. Engagement with the alcohol-related video was linked to craving. This study aimed to replicate these findings three years later outside the lockdown context.
A hundred and forty-two psychology students were randomly allocated to either a “pre-post” or a “post-only” craving assessment group. They watched an alcohol-related video. SD, demand effects, engagement, and alcohol consumption levels were also assessed. 
Several findings supported the robustness of effects observed in the initial study, while others diverged, offering new hypotheses. Heavier drinkers reported significantly higher craving levels following video exposure and craving correlated moderately with video engagement. No significant associations were found between craving and SD or demand characteristics. However, unlike the initial study, repeated measurements showed no significant effects or interactions with alcohol consumption.
Reanalysis of the original data highlighted a gender-based interaction, where the effect of the interaction between repeated craving measurements and alcohol consumption was significant in men but not in women. These results suggest partial replication of prior findings and underscore the need to further explore contextual and demographic influences on alcohol craving. Moreover, this study confirmed a significant role of video engagement in craving.


Contact e-mail: equertemont@uliege.be or m.heck@uliege.be

he7rn.title: Daily fluctuations in delay discounting and smartphone-based multimodal measures track nighttime drinking in alcohol use disorder: A 28-day monitoring study
he7rn.description:
Background and Aims:
Alcohol use disorder (AUD) involves day-to-day fluctuations in cognitive, psychological, and contextual factors that are difficult to fully capture using retrospective or cross-sectional assessment alone. Computational markers, such as discounting rate, ambiguity tolerance, and risk preference, may provide useful indicators of day-level drinking risk, but have rarely been assessed repeatedly in real-world settings or examined together with daily app-based self-reports and passive smartphone-derived measures. This study examined whether these multimodal day-level measures were associated with same-day nighttime drinking, focusing on whether within-person changes in computational markers remained associated with drinking after accounting for concurrent self-reported and contextual measures.

Design:
A 28-day longitudinal observational study using repeated smartphone-based assessments and passive sensing.

Setting:
Daily-life smartphone-based monitoring of community-recruited participants in the Republic of Korea.

Participants:
The final analytic sample included 143 adults meeting DSM-5 criteria for AUD.

Measurements:
The primary outcome was same-day nighttime drinking, assessed using daily app-based self-reports. Daily predictors covered three domains. App-based self-reports included mood, alcohol craving, meal intake, and sleep-related variables. Computational predictors were derived from delay discounting and choice under risk and ambiguity tasks. Passive smartphone-derived predictors included time spent at home, calls, steps, and foreground app use. Generalized linear mixed-effects models examined associations between nighttime drinking and multimodal predictors decomposed into within-person and between-person components.

Findings:
A multimodal model integrating computational parameters, daily app-based self-reports, and passive smartphone-derived measures showed the highest explanatory power for same-day nighttime drinking (marginal R² = 0.22). In this final model, higher within-person delay discounting [log(k)], alcohol craving, and positive mood, as well as lower meal intake and percent time at home, were associated with a higher likelihood of nighttime drinking. At the between-person level, only lower average meal intake was associated with a higher likelihood of nighttime drinking. Within-person fluctuations in log(k) were largely uncorrelated with other day-level predictors and showed no clear next-day association.

Conclusions:
Associations with same-day nighttime drinking were observed primarily at the within-person level, suggesting that day-level changes may be more informative than stable individual differences. Within-person increases in delay discounting remained associated with nighttime drinking after accounting for other modalities, suggesting that multimodal monitoring may help identify periods of elevated drinking risk.

kjerd.title: Recommendations for Recruiting Clinically-Relevant Stratified Samples Across Race/Ethnicity, Gender, and Socioeconomic Status Using the Prolific Online Platform
kjerd.description: Though online sampling methods have progressed, psychology literature continues to include primarily white, educated, upper-class samples. This work will outline the methodology of a stratified community study examining demographic differences in fear of happiness and depression among U.S. adults. Drawing on prior research suggesting variation in emotional processes across social identities, we implemented a fully crossed stratified sampling design to ensure equal representation across race/ethnicity (Black, Asian, Latine, White), gender (cisgender male, cisgender female), and socioeconomic status (SES). Eligible participants were 18-65 years old and residing in the U.S. SES categories were defined as ≤ $50,000 (low), $50,000 - $100,000 (middle), and ≥ $100,000 (high). A priori power analysis (G*Power) indicated a target of 42 participants per cell (N=1,008) to detect medium effects. Following the exclusion of 75 uncategorizable cases, the per-cell target was adjusted to 38, maintaining adequate power for medium-to-large effects. Participants were recruited in SES-based blocks via Prolific. Procedures to preserve stratification, confidentiality, and analytic rigor will be discussed, highlighting methodological considerations for equity-focused psychological research. These methods can ensure adequate power and representation when examining relationships between intersectional identities and psychological phenomena in online samples.

8j7n2.title: Coordinated Psychological Intervention Ensembles Outperform Single-Mechanism Designs Across Societal Challenges
8j7n2.description: Behavioral science tends to seek the single most predictive explanation for behavioral phenomena and subsequently the most effective intervention for behavior change. Yet many consequential outcomes, from climate action to democratic attitudes, result from multiple psychological processes, raising the question of whether combining interventions that leverage different processes would prove more effective. Across a 13-intervention megastudy and a second, megastudy-informed experiment (Ns = 8,488 and 2,547 US adults; both quota-matched and preregistered) we test whether intervention ensembles—deliberate combinations of distinct psychological modules delivered together—can more effectively shape target behaviors and attitudes.  We test three principles for constructing such ensembles: 1) outcome optimizing ensembles (combining top-performing individual interventions), 2) mechanism coverage ensembles (covering key psychological drivers of the outcome), and 3) multi-level synergy ensembles (combining individual-, collective-, and system-level processes). Ensembles consistently outperformed their constituent single-mechanism interventions. In Study 1, relative to an informed control, the strongest ensemble increased climate policy support by 41.5% and donation by 43.0%. And in Study 2, the ensemble was significantly more effective than its components in reducing partisan animosity and strengthening democratic attitudes. The largest gains were achieved by multi-level synergistic ensembles that aligned processes across individual, collective, and systemic levels. LLM-assisted analyses of participant reasoning reveal that effective ensembles work by resolving complementary psychological barriers, not by simply providing more arguments. For complex societal challenges shaped by multiple barriers, interventions can achieve behavior change at greater rate and scale by designing coordinated intervention ensembles rather than by searching for the single best mechanism.

fme25.title: Can a yoga psychology intervention change primal world beliefs?
fme25.description: Worldviews play a central role in psychological well-being, yet little research has examined whether fundamental world beliefs (“primals”) can shift following psychological intervention. The present exploratory study examined whether a yoga psychology based 12-session long intervention was associated with changes in primal world beliefs and well-being amongst adults diagnosed with mild-to-moderate depressive disorder (N = 31). Using a single-group pre–post design, participants completed measures of well-being, depressive symptoms, primal world beliefs and Triguna. Results indicated significant improvements in well-being across multiple measures (all p < .001), accompanied by increases in positive primal beliefs (Safe, Enticing, Alive, and Good) and changes in Triguna (increased sattva alongside reductions in rajas and tamas). Informant ratings of triguna traits paralleled self-reports, suggesting observable change. Depressive symptoms also decreased significantly. Findings provide preliminary evidence that shifts in fundamental beliefs toward the world may occur, along with an increase in well-being.

mwfah.title: The robustness of implicit evaluation updating: Tests of time course and resistance to the return of negativity
mwfah.description: Accumulating evidence suggests that even highly negative implicit (automatic) social evaluations are amenable to temporary shifts toward positivity; however, demonstrations of long-term change, especially in the negative-to-positive direction, are rare. Here we report 10 experiments (9 preregistered; total N = 3,103) probing whether negative-to-positive reversals in implicit evaluations can persist over time and are robust to subsequent negative learning. To this end, we first identified two procedures involving diagnostic behavioral information (reinterpretation and negation + replacement) that, when measured immediately following the intervention, overturned experimentally created implicit negativity toward novel social targets, as measured by the Affect Misattribution Procedure (AMP; Experiments 1–2B). Attesting to the durability of this effect, negative-to-positive reversals persisted without any decrement over a 2-day delay (Experiment 3). Exposure to subsequent negative information on day 3 caused a return of implicit evaluations to negativity, indicating vulnerability to new learning (Experiments 4–5). However, critically, this shift did not reflect unique potentiation of negative learning by initial negative information: The negativity emerging in the positive-to-negative updating condition was statistically equivalent to that following univalent positive initial learning (Experiments 6A–6C) or in the absence of any prior learning (Experiment 7). Demonstrating the flexibility of automatic evaluative processing, these data suggest that negative-to-positive implicit evaluation change can be both durable over time and robust against the potentiation of subsequent negative learning. We discuss implications for social cognitive theory and the prospect of durably changing well-established implicit evaluations of known targets.

4s9jk.title: Promoting well-doing: A Positive Psychology intervention for regulating performance momentum and thriving among high achievers
4s9jk.description: The present study examined a novel journalling intervention designed to support subjective wellbeing and perceived work performance among high achievers. Sixty-seven university students reporting a grade average of B+ or higher completed a one-week, within-subjects pre–post study involving a daily ‘well done’ journalling task focused on small actions done well. Subjective wellbeing and perceived work performance were measured pre- and post- intervention; with journalling frequency and qualitative feedback collected afterwards. Findings indicated no detectable change in wellbeing from an elevated baseline, however, a significant improvement in perceived work performance was observed. Post-hoc analyses revealed a dose–response pattern, with stronger performance improvements among participants with frequent task engagement. Qualitative feedback was generally favourable, suggesting perceived benefits in positive affect, self-esteem, and motivation. Overall, results show preliminary support for ‘well done’ journalling to enhance perceived performance in high-achieving students.

2c8xf.title: Using features of dynamic networks to guide treatment selection and outcome prediction: The central role of uncertainty
2c8xf.description: Multivariate time series models are commonly used in psychology to investigate person-specific associations between multiple variables. They are often represented and interpreted as dynamic network models, where features such as the centrality of nodes can potentially guide treatment selection and outcome prediction. Researchers typically rely on point estimates of specific network features while ignoring estimation uncertainty, which can lead to wrong inferences and over-optimistic claims.  We introduce a one-step Bayesian approach for estimating multilevel vector autoregressive models (BmlVAR), which enables uncertainty quantification for person-specific network features and the regression of external outcomes on such features. In a preregistered simulation study, we compare the new model with several popular methods for network estimation. We also apply all methods to empirical data to highlight their differences. Our simulation results indicate that all methods perform mediocrely in estimating different centrality measures in practically relevant settings. BmlVAR still outperforms the other methods in many simulation conditions, especially regarding the statistical power to detect associations with an outcome. However, estimating the model properly can be challenging with limited data. Overall, all methods require a lot of data or very large effects to produce reasonably accurate results. Thus, although centrality measures based on dynamic networks have been widely used, our simulation study suggests they are unlikely to work well for achieving major goals of interest, such as guiding treatment selection and outcome prediction. We provide a new model that incorporates estimation uncertainty into the modelling process, thereby protecting against premature conclusions.

uz3x5.title: “Already Outdated and Still Under Review”:  Mapping the Landscape of Research on Adolescent Development and AI Chatbot Use
uz3x5.description: Artificial intelligence (AI) chatbots are increasingly embedded in adolescents’ daily lives, yet developmental science research struggles to keep pace with adoption. This mixed-methods study gathered developmental expert perspectives via a survey (n = 141 researchers and adjacent professionals working on issues related to youth and technology) and semi-structured interviews with a purposive subsample (n = 15) to inform a coordinated research and policy agenda on adolescent AI use. The study aims were to (1) identify priority AI use cases for future study, (2) identify priority research questions and methods, as well as barriers to future research, and (3) identify societal guardrails that developmental professionals recommend enacting now, while research is underway. Experts cautioned against vague "screen time" measures and agreed that nearly all use cases merit study but prioritized those with severe or irreversible immediate-term harms, AI literacy, AI as a social/relational actor, AI-generated (mis)information, and overreliance. No single methodological consensus emerged; participants advocated broadly for methodological pluralism and triangulation. Key barriers included the rapid pace of change in AI technology and use relative to academic timelines, limited industry transparency, funding gaps, and misaligned academic incentives. Proposed solutions included shared infrastructure, interdisciplinary consortia, and community partnerships. Experts underscored that responsibility for safe AI should not rest primarily on adolescents and families and identified three immediate guardrails: enforceable youth-centered regulation, developmentally informed product design, and multidimensional AI literacy. Findings clarify a preliminary direction for future research and advocacy and emphasize that coordination among researchers and between sectors is needed.

7s2e9.title: Trait Apathy and Fatigue are Associated with Diminished Academic Functioning Among University Students Aged 30 - 50
7s2e9.description: Motivation is critical to successful academic functioning. A growing body of evidence details how motivation evolves throughout the lifetime, yet middle adulthood is rarely examined. Here we examined how trait apathy and fatigue, both of which manifest in diminished motivation, relate to self-reported academic functioning among university students aged 30 – 50. 332 students completed trait apathy and fatigue scales, measures of academic functioning (autonomous motivation, controlled motivation, study engagement, study effort, academic stress, dropout intentions, academic performance, and study satisfaction), and measures of family, household, work-related factors. Apathy and fatigue were related to almost all academic measures, yet apathy was more closely linked to academic functioning; in particular autonomous motivation, study engagement, and study satisfaction. Our results suggest that relatively stable individual differences in motivation among healthy adults, as captured by the measures of trait-apathy and fatigue, are closely linked to functioning in a critical area – academic development.
